decade i · who we are

Preachers in ordinary life

We are a chapter of the Lay Fraternities of Saint Dominic — men and women who live the 800-year-old Dominican charism in the middle of family, work, and parish life. Our vocation is summed up in a single phrase from Saint Thomas: contemplata aliis tradere — to contemplate, and to hand on to others the fruits of contemplation.

Our chapter

Our history

[Placeholder — a short paragraph on when your chapter was founded, its patron, and how it came to be. Two to four sentences is plenty; I can draft this with you once the group settles the details.]

Come and see

For those discerning

If you sense a pull toward deeper prayer and serious study — but your vocation is lived in the world, not the cloister — the Lay Dominicans may be the home you're looking for. Come sit in on a meeting. No commitment; just come and see.

the four pillars

Four pillars, one life

Everything a Lay Dominican does rests on four supports. Keep scrolling.

Pillar I

Prayer

The Liturgy of the Hours, daily Rosary, and time before the Blessed Sacrament anchor everything else.

Pray with us →
Pillar II

Study

Truth is our motto. We study Scripture, Aquinas, and the tradition — not as scholars, but as disciples.

Sit with the doctors →
Pillar III

Community

We meet monthly as a chapter: fraternity, formation, and friendship across every age and walk of life.

Join a meeting →
Pillar IV

Apostolate

What we receive, we give — through teaching, presentations, service, and witness in our own towns.

See what we're building →

gather with us

Calendar & class times

All are welcome at our open events. Inquirers are especially encouraged to attend a chapter meeting.

3rd Saturday · monthly

Chapter Meeting

Morning prayer, a formation talk, and fraternity — with formation classes following the meeting. Inquirers are always welcome.

Every Friday · after morning Mass

Eucharistic Adoration

Silent adoration before the Blessed Sacrament, continuing through the day; end time varies. All are welcome to come and pray.

decade ii · the real presence

Eucharistic miracles

In every Mass, bread and wine truly become the Body and Blood of Christ. A handful of times in history, the Lord has let that hidden reality become visible — and modern science has been permitted to look.

Lanciano, Italy · 8th century

The oldest recorded miracle

A monk doubting the Real Presence saw the host become flesh and the wine become blood during Mass. Studies in the 1970s identified the flesh as human cardiac tissue, blood type AB — still incorrupt after twelve centuries.

Buenos Aires, Argentina · 1996

Investigated under Cardinal Bergoglio

A discarded host placed in water began to show what appeared to be blood and tissue. Independent laboratory analysis, commissioned by the future Pope Francis, identified inflamed human heart muscle.

Sokółka, Poland · 2008

A miracle under the microscope

A dropped host developed a red mark that pathologists at the Medical University of Białystok concluded was heart-muscle tissue, interwoven with the structure of the bread in a way they could not explain.

Blessed Carlo Acutis — the teenager beatified in 2020 — spent his short life cataloguing these miracles online. His exhibition documents over 150 of them, and it remains the definitive resource.

Visit Carlo's exhibition →

decade iii · our lady

Church-approved Marian apparitions

The Church investigates claimed apparitions with deep caution; only a small number have received formal approval. These are among them — and in each, Our Lady's message converges: prayer, penance, and her Son.

Guadalupe, Mexico · 1531

Our Lady of Guadalupe

Mary appeared to Saint Juan Diego and left her image miraculously imprinted on his tilma — an image that led to the conversion of millions and still defies full scientific explanation.

Basilica of Guadalupe →
Lourdes, France · 1858

Our Lady of Lourdes

To Saint Bernadette, Mary announced: "I am the Immaculate Conception." The spring at the grotto has been the site of seventy-plus cures the Church has formally recognized as medically inexplicable.

Sanctuary of Lourdes →
Knock, Ireland · 1879

Our Lady of Knock

A silent apparition: Mary, Saint Joseph, Saint John, and the Lamb upon an altar, witnessed by fifteen villagers in the rain. Not one word was spoken — the Eucharistic image was the message.

Knock Shrine →
Fatima, Portugal · 1917

Our Lady of Fatima

To three shepherd children, Mary asked for the daily Rosary and penance for sinners — and confirmed it with the Miracle of the Sun, witnessed by tens of thousands.

Sanctuary of Fatima →
Champion, Wisconsin · 1859

Our Lady of Good Help

The only Church-approved Marian apparition on United States soil. To a young Belgian immigrant, Mary said simply: "Gather the children... and teach them what they should know for salvation."

National Shrine →

decade iv · the order of preachers

Dominican saints

Eight centuries of holiness: founders and mystics, doctors of the Church and servants of the poor. These are our family — and their writings are our inheritance.

St. Dominic de Guzmán

1170–1221 · Founder

He spoke only to God or about God. Founding the Order of Preachers to combat error with truth and charity, Dominic wed rigorous study to itinerant preaching — and tradition holds he received the Rosary from Our Lady herself.

Life of St. Dominic →

St. Catherine of Siena

1347–1380 · Doctor of the Church · Lay Dominican

A lay Dominican — like us — who counseled popes, ended a papal exile, and left us The Dialogue, one of the great works of Western mysticism. Proof that profession in the world is no obstacle to sanctity.

Read The Dialogue →

St. Thomas Aquinas

1225–1274 · Doctor of the Church

The Angelic Doctor, whose Summa Theologiae remains the Church's greatest single synthesis of faith and reason. Near the end of his life, after a vision at Mass, he said all he had written seemed as straw compared with what he had seen.

Read the Summa →

St. Martin de Porres

1579–1639 · Lay brother

Born in Lima to a Spanish father and a freed African mother, Martin joined the Dominicans as a lay helper and became the city's healer of the poor — famed for humility, miracles, and a tenderness that extended even to the mice in the priory.

Canonization homily →

St. Rose of Lima

1586–1617 · Lay Dominican

The first canonized saint of the Americas — a Dominican tertiary who lived heroic prayer and penance from a garden hermitage behind her family home, supporting her parents by her needlework all the while.

Her story →

the crucifix · our devotion

The Holy Rosary

You have been scrolling along its beads this whole time. The Rosary is the Gospel in miniature: twenty scenes from the life of Christ, contemplated through the eyes of His mother, carried on the rhythm of familiar prayers.

Joyful Mysteries

Mondays & Saturdays
  1. The Annunciation
  2. The Visitation
  3. The Nativity
  4. The Presentation
  5. Finding Jesus in the Temple

Sorrowful Mysteries

Tuesdays & Fridays
  1. The Agony in the Garden
  2. The Scourging at the Pillar
  3. The Crowning with Thorns
  4. The Carrying of the Cross
  5. The Crucifixion

Glorious Mysteries

Wednesdays & Sundays
  1. The Resurrection
  2. The Ascension
  3. The Descent of the Holy Spirit
  4. The Assumption
  5. The Coronation of Mary

Luminous Mysteries

Thursdays
  1. The Baptism in the Jordan
  2. The Wedding at Cana
  3. Proclamation of the Kingdom
  4. The Transfiguration
  5. Institution of the Eucharist

New to the Rosary? Come pray it with us — see the calendar for our weekly chapter Rosary. It's easier learned in company than from a page.

decade v · contemplata aliis tradere

Our work

Study in our chapter isn't private. Members research, write, and present — and we publish that work here, both to teach and to show inquirers what a life of Dominican study actually looks like.

Resources for formation

The tools our members actually use — free or nearly free, and faithful.

video course

Aquinas 101

Free video courses on St. Thomas from the Dominican friars of the Thomistic Institute. The best on-ramp to the Summa that exists.

aquinas101.thomisticinstitute.org →
app · liturgy of the hours

Divine Office

Pray the Liturgy of the Hours — the daily prayer of the Church and of every Dominican — with audio and text.

divineoffice.org →
app · breviary

iBreviary

The complete breviary and missal, free, in a dozen languages. A workhorse for daily prayer on the go.

ibreviary.org →
podcast

Godsplaining

Dominican friars of the Province of St. Joseph talking theology, culture, and the spiritual life — our Order's own voice in your earbuds.

godsplaining.org →
app · prayer

Hallow

Guided Rosaries, Lectio Divina, and daily meditations. A gentle way in for anyone building a habit of prayer.

hallow.com →
the order

The Order of Preachers

The worldwide Dominican family — news, history, and the lives of our saints, from the Order itself.

op.org →
